APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: event factuality is a core NLP task and the paper uses contemporary neural methods (attention-based CNN, BiLSTM+CNN) and standard evaluation on FactBank. The evaluation protocol (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro averages, significance testing) and comparisons to rule-based and MaxEnt baselines match venue expectations. Minor scope concerns are the restricted evaluation to five factuality categories and limited candidate token selection (nouns/verbs/adjectives), which may limit broad impact but do not preclude fit for a top venue. | B(5): This is squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: event factuality is a core semantic NLP task and the paper targets the right audience. It employs contemporary neural methods (attention-based CNN, BiLSTM+CNN) and evaluates on a standard dataset (FactBank) against reasonable baselines with standard metrics (precision/recall/F1 and significance testing). The problem formulation (two-step extraction of SIPs/sources/cues followed by neural classification) and evaluation protocol match expectations for such venues. The stated limitations (restricted label set, candidate token types, some unavailable features) are noted but do not undermine topical fit or relevance.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and logical (intro → method describing the two-step supervised framework and attention-based models → experiments on FactBank → conclusion), and its main contributions are stated early (SIP detection via attention-CNN and a BiLSTM+CNN factuality model). Writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage is adequate, which supports readability. However, the provided summary does not mention notation consistency or the effectiveness of figures/tables/examples, so minor presentation gaps may remain unassessed. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from framework description to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (two-step framework and attention-based models) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined, with hyperparameters and evaluation metrics reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples support the evaluation (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro averages) and the writing quality is described as good; minor presentation issues remain (e.g., clear statement of evaluation limitations such as the five factuality categories and candidate-token restrictions).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against established baselines (Saurí 2008 rule-based, de Marneffe et al. 2012 MaxEnt) and a CNN+CNN variant on the FactBank dataset using standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and significance testing. However, comparisons may be partly unfair because some features used by the MaxEnt baseline are unavailable in this system (they rely on annotated information not produced by the paper’s basic factor extraction), which could disadvantage that baseline. The authors report hyperparameters, acknowledge limitations, and cite related work adequately, indicating mostly comprehensive but not perfectly fair comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares against established and relevant baselines (Saurí 2008 rule-based model and the MaxEnt model of de Marneffe et al. 2012) and evaluates on the same FactBank dataset with standard metrics and significance testing, which supports largely fair comparisons. However, fairness is partly compromised because some features used by the MaxEnt baseline were unavailable (they rely on annotated information not produced by the authors' basic factor extraction), and there is no mention of more recent neural factuality approaches as direct baselines. The authors do analyze architectural variants (e.g., CNN+CNN) and report statistical significance, and related work coverage is described as adequate, so the comparisons are good overall but with minor omissions and one fairness caveat.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against established baselines (Saurí 2008 rule-based, de Marneffe et al. 2012 MaxEnt) and a CNN+CNN variant on the FactBank dataset using standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and significance testing. However, comparisons may be partly unfair because some features used by the MaxEnt baseline are unavailable in this system (they rely on annotated information not produced by the paper’s basic factor extraction), which could disadvantage that baseline. The authors report hyperparameters, acknowledge limitations, and cite related work adequately, indicating mostly comprehensive but not perfectly fair comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against established and relevant baselines (Saurí 2008 rule-based model and the MaxEnt model of de Marneffe et al. 2012) and evaluates on the same FactBank dataset with standard metrics and significance testing, which supports largely fair comparisons. However, fairness is partly compromised because some features used by the MaxEnt baseline were unavailable (they rely on annotated information not produced by the authors' basic factor extraction), and there is no mention of more recent neural factuality approaches as direct baselines. The authors do analyze architectural variants (e.g., CNN+CNN) and report statistical significance, and related work coverage is described as adequate, so the comparisons are good overall but with minor omissions and one fairness caveat.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments are adequate in comparing against rule-based, MaxEnt and a CNN+CNN variant on FactBank with detailed metrics and significance testing, but evaluation appears limited to a single dataset which weakens generality. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is minimal (only a CNN+CNN variant mentioned) and there is no evidence of systematic component-wise ablations or robustness checks. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations/failure modes are explicitly discussed (limited factuality categories, candidate token types, missing baseline features, class imbalance). Reproducibility is partial: hyperparameters are reported but code is not mentioned and some features used by baselines are unavailable, making full replication difficult. | B(4): Experiments compare the proposed two-step system against reasonable baselines (Rules, MaxEnt, CNN+CNN) on the FactBank dataset using multiple metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and significance testing, which largely supports the claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited — a CNN+CNN variant is tested but no extensive ablation of components or robustness checks are reported. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations (limited label set, restricted candidate tokens, missing baseline features, class imbalance) are explicitly discussed; hyperparameters are reported but code is not mentioned, reducing full reproducibility. Overall the evaluation is solid with some minor gaps in analysis depth and reproducibility details.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate in comparing against rule-based, MaxEnt and a CNN+CNN variant on FactBank with detailed metrics and significance testing, but evaluation appears limited to a single dataset which weakens generality. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is minimal (only a CNN+CNN variant mentioned) and there is no evidence of systematic component-wise ablations or robustness checks. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations/failure modes are explicitly discussed (limited factuality categories, candidate token types, missing baseline features, class imbalance). Reproducibility is partial: hyperparameters are reported but code is not mentioned and some features used by baselines are unavailable, making full replication difficult.

Related work is described as adequate and several limitations (limited label set, restricted candidate tokens, missing baseline features, class imbalance) are explicitly discussed; hyperparameters are reported but code is not mentioned, reducing full reproducibility. Overall the evaluation is solid with some minor gaps in analysis depth and reproducibility details.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a two-step supervised framework and introduces task-specific uses of attention-based CNNs for SIP detection and an attention-equipped BiLSTM+CNN model operating over shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs. These contributions are clearly differentiated from prior rules (Saurí 2008) and MaxEnt feature-based baselines, showing a domain-tailored system design. However, the core techniques (attention, CNN, BiLSTM, shortest dependency paths) are existing methods combined in a fairly straightforward way for factuality, so the novelty is moderate and mainly incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea. | B(3): The paper combines existing neural components (attention, CNN, BiLSTM) in a two-step pipeline—an attention-based CNN for Source Introducing Predicate detection followed by an attention BiLSTM+CNN over shortest dependency paths—to tackle event factuality. These design choices appear to be a reasonably non-trivial engineering synthesis and are clearly differentiated from prior rule-based (Saurí 2008) and MaxEnt (de Marneffe et al. 2012) baselines, but they primarily apply well-known architectures to a well-studied factuality dataset (FactBank) rather than introducing fundamentally new methods or formulations. Overall this reads as an incremental but solidly differentiated contribution rather than a highly original conceptual advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a two-step supervised framework and introduces task-specific uses of attention-based CNNs for SIP detection and an attention-equipped BiLSTM+CNN model operating over shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs. These contributions are clearly differentiated from prior rules (Saurí 2008) and MaxEnt feature-based baselines, showing a domain-tailored system design. However, the core techniques (attention, CNN, BiLSTM, shortest dependency paths) are existing methods combined in a fairly straightforward way for factuality, so the novelty is moderate and mainly incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines existing neural components (attention, CNN, BiLSTM) in a two-step pipeline—an attention-based CNN for Source Introducing Predicate detection followed by an attention BiLSTM+CNN over shortest dependency paths—to tackle event factuality. These design choices appear to be a reasonably non-trivial engineering synthesis and are clearly differentiated from prior rule-based (Saurí 2008) and MaxEnt (de Marneffe et al. 2012) baselines, but they primarily apply well-known architectures to a well-studied factuality dataset (FactBank) rather than introducing fundamentally new methods or formulations. Overall this reads as an incremental but solidly differentiated contribution rather than a highly original conceptual advance.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles the important task of event factuality identification and introduces a two-step supervised framework that combines an attention-based CNN for detecting Source Introducing Predicates with an attention-equipped BiLSTM+CNN model operating over shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs. Evaluation on the standard FactBank dataset and comparisons to rule-based and MaxEnt baselines suggest the approach is likely to influence researchers exploring neural architectures for factuality. However, restricted evaluation to five factuality categories, narrow candidate token selection, and omitted baseline features limit its broader immediate impact, so the contribution is moderately influential rather than transformative. | B(3): The paper tackles an important task—event factuality identification—by proposing a two-step supervised framework that includes an attention-based CNN for Source Introducing Predicate detection and an attention-driven BiLSTM+CNN model over shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs. Evaluation on the established FactBank dataset and comparisons to rule-based and MaxEnt baselines suggest the approach is practically relevant. However, the impact is moderated by scope limitations (evaluation restricted to five factuality categories, candidate tokens limited to nouns/verbs/adjectives, and missing features used by some baselines), so it is likely to be a useful, but not field-changing, contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important task of event factuality identification and introduces a two-step supervised framework that combines an attention-based CNN for detecting Source Introducing Predicates with an attention-equipped BiLSTM+CNN model operating over shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs. Evaluation on the standard FactBank dataset and comparisons to rule-based and MaxEnt baselines suggest the approach is likely to influence researchers exploring neural architectures for factuality. However, restricted evaluation to five factuality categories, narrow candidate token selection, and omitted baseline features limit its broader immediate impact, so the contribution is moderately influential rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important task—event factuality identification—by proposing a two-step supervised framework that includes an attention-based CNN for Source Introducing Predicate detection and an attention-driven BiLSTM+CNN model over shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs. Evaluation on the established FactBank dataset and comparisons to rule-based and MaxEnt baselines suggest the approach is practically relevant. However, the impact is moderated by scope limitations (evaluation restricted to five factuality categories, candidate tokens limited to nouns/verbs/adjectives, and missing features used by some baselines), so it is likely to be a useful, but not field-changing, contribution.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evaluation on FactBank against reasonable baselines (rule-based Saurí 2008, MaxEnt from de Marneffe et al., and a CNN+CNN ablation) using standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and two-sample two-tailed t-tests. There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and none are reported. A methodological caveat is that the MaxEnt baseline could not use some annotated features from prior work, which weakens direct comparability, and evaluation is limited to five factuality categories and candidate tokens (nouns, verbs, adjectives), reducing generality. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the experimental support for the main conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically evaluated on the FactBank dataset against reasonable baselines (Rules, MaxEnt, CNN+CNN) with standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and significance testing (two-sample two-tailed t-test), which supports the proposed two-step neural framework. There are no mathematical derivations to critique, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the experimental comparison to the MaxEnt baseline is weakened because some of its features (predicate/event classes) were unavailable and thus not used, and the evaluation is limited to five factuality categories and to nouns/verbs/adjectives as candidate events, which may limit generality. Overall the methodology is sound but these limitations introduce minor concerns about the breadth and fairness of the evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evaluation on FactBank against reasonable baselines (rule-based Saurí 2008, MaxEnt from de Marneffe et al., and a CNN+CNN ablation) using standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and two-sample two-tailed t-tests. There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and none are reported. A methodological caveat is that the MaxEnt baseline could not use some annotated features from prior work, which weakens direct comparability, and evaluation is limited to five factuality categories and candidate tokens (nouns, verbs, adjectives), reducing generality. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the experimental support for the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically evaluated on the FactBank dataset against reasonable baselines (Rules, MaxEnt, CNN+CNN) with standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and significance testing (two-sample two-tailed t-test), which supports the proposed two-step neural framework. There are no mathematical derivations to critique, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the experimental comparison to the MaxEnt baseline is weakened because some of its features (predicate/event classes) were unavailable and thus not used, and the evaluation is limited to five factuality categories and to nouns/verbs/adjectives as candidate events, which may limit generality. Overall the methodology is sound but these limitations introduce minor concerns about the breadth and fairness of the evaluation.

main_claims: Proposes a two-step supervised framework for identifying event factuality in raw texts.
Utilizes an attention-based CNN to detect Source Introducing Predicates (SIPs).
Proposes an attention-based deep neural network combining BiLSTM and CNN to identify event factuality from shortest dependency paths and lexical inputs.
_summary.methodology: A two-step system first extracts basic factors (SIPs, sources, cues) from raw text, then applies an attention-based neural model that uses BiLSTM over syntactic paths and CNN over words (with lexical features and attention) to classify event factuality.
baselines: MaxEnt (maximum entropy model using features from de Marneffe et al. 2012)
limitations: Evaluation is limited to five factuality categories (CT+, CT-, PR+, PS+, Uu) only.
Event detection considers only nouns, verbs, and adjectives as candidate tokens.
Some features used by baseline MaxEnt (e.g., predicate classes and general classes of events) are not available because they rely on annotated information not produced by the basic factor extraction.
Class imbalance: negative and speculative factuality values are in the minority, motivating the two-output design.
